response:
I read ISSUE-106.md and both probe reports, then grounded the call against the actual repo (checked `check-pr-body.mjs`/`gates.yml` — ADR 0030's precedent — and confirmed via grep that `claude -p` is invoked ad hoc across a dozen disconnected harnesses with no common dispatch wrapper). Decision recorded as `docs/decisions/0045-sync-before-spend-pr-body-gate.md`:

---

```
---
id: 0045
title: "Sync-before-spend ships as a PR-body gate line, not a claim protocol or dispatch hook"
status: accepted
summary: "Issue #30 ran twice (~$5-8 wasted, self-corrected by luck) because nothing forced a re-sync before spend. Ship sync-before-spend as a required `Sync-check:` PR-body line mirroring ADR 0030's Retrospective line, enforced by extending check-pr-body.mjs's presence gate — reachable now, no new infrastructure. Reject the issue-comment claim protocol (permission + non-atomicity holes ISSUE-106.md names itself) and a pre-flight dispatch hook (no common entry point exists: `claude -p` runs ad hoc across a dozen disconnected harnesses) as premature machinery for a rare, cheap, self-correcting failure class (closes #106)."
---
```

**Decision:** Ship sync-before-spend as a required PR-body line, `Sync-check: <issue#> re-verified open, no closing PR found (<timestamp>)`, produced immediately before opening the PR — the same workflow slot as ADR 0030's `Retrospective:` line. Extend `scripts/check-pr-body.mjs`'s already-tested presence gate with a second anchored regex. Reject the issue-comment claim protocol and a dispatch pre-flight hook for now.

**Why:** the cheapest lever that's still a real gate, not prose. It closes the premortem's strongest finding (unenforced prose decays — true by this repo's own forcing-function philosophy, and concretely true here because context summarization erodes an early-session "repeat before push" instruction by the time a long session actually pushes) without needing issue-write permission (sidesteps the claim protocol's named permission hole) or new dispatch infrastructure (sidesteps the assumption probe's reversing premise for a pre-flight hook — verified there's no common entry point today).

**The single load-bearing assumption** (subject matter, not process): duplicate-spend collisions in this repo's casual backlog-dispatch class stay rare and cheap enough that a self-attested pre-push check — not an atomic lock — is proportionate. This is distinct from ADR 0042's already-covered deliberate paid-experiment class.

**The failure class accepted:** a narrowed-but-not-eliminated race. Two sessions dispatched close enough in time can still both pass a pre-push sync-check before either pushes (no atomicity), or the check itself gets rubber-stamped under pressure rather than genuinely re-verified — reproducing the original incident at the point of merge instead of at dispatch. If a second incident occurs despite the line, or a collision lands inside ADR 0042's cost-pilot regime where the spend no longer self-corrects, this reverses toward a real claim/lock mechanism (revisit triggers in the ADR).

**Rejected:** claim protocol (permission-withholding + non-atomic check-then-act + orphaned-claim ownership gap, all named in the issue/premortem); dispatch pre-flight hook (no reachable attach point exists yet — confirmed by grep, `claude -p` is invoked ad hoc across a dozen disconnected harnesses); prose-only (the issue names its own weakness); do-nothing (the incident's self-correction was luck, not design, per the issue itself).

---

Note: I couldn't run `adr-lint.mjs` or `git fetch` in this session — those commands required approval that wasn't granted. Please run `node pdca-workflow/scripts/adr-lint.mjs docs/decisions` before merging to confirm frontmatter/budget/falsifiability pass, and confirm 0045 is still the next free ID against `origin/main`.

premortem.response:
## Premortem: "sync-before-spend rule enough, or mechanize now?" — assume it shipped and failed by 2027-01

**1. The prose rule decays because nothing forces it — the repo's own philosophy predicts this.** CLAUDE.md:23 ("Forcing functions: `/retrospect` before every PR; `adr-lint` guards the decision log...") establishes that this repo only trusts rules backed by a script or gate; ISSUE-106.md's own candidate list flags the sync-before-spend option as having "the same enforcement weakness as any prose rule." If the decision ships as CLAUDE.md text alone, the failure mode is the original incident repeating verbatim: a session re-syncs at dispatch (satisfying the letter of the rule) but the "repeat before the final push" clause — the part that actually would have caught issue #30's collision — gets skipped under budget/context pressure on a long-running session, because no lint or CI step checks whether the second sync happened. Six months out, someone finds a second duplicate-spend and the postmortem reads identically to this one.

**2. The claim protocol gets built but silently fails to fire because dispatch instructions withhold issue-write permission.** ISSUE-106.md names this directly: the claim protocol "requires permission to touch issues, which dispatch instructions sometimes withhold ('don't change the issues')." If "claim" or "both" is chosen, the mechanism is only as strong as its weakest dispatch config — any backlog-dispatch prompt that doesn't grant issue-comment permission produces a session that either can't post the claim (and proceeds anyway, since nothing blocks execution on a failed claim-post) or errors and is worked around by a human who just tells it to continue. Because dispatch prompts are written ad hoc per session rather than centrally enforced, this isn't a rare edge case — it's the default for any dispatcher who copies an older "don't touch issues" prompt, and the collision returns exactly there.

**3. The claim itself isn't atomic, so the mechanism narrows the race window without closing it — and stale claims erode trust in it.** A "post a comment when starting" claim is a check-then-act step over a network round-trip (GitHub API), not a lock: two sessions dispatched within the same window can both read "no claim" before either posts one, reproducing #30's race at a smaller scale. Separately, nothing in the candidate list specifies who clears a claim left behind by a session that crashes or stalls — and CLAUDE.md's own related lesson ("a detached-background subagent stalled twice until nudged," noted in ISSUE-106.md's closing paragraph) shows sessions do stall silently in this repo. An orphaned "in progress" comment either blocks a legitimate second attempt indefinitely or, once enough stale claims accumulate, trains future sessions to treat "in progress" as noise and ignore it — reintroducing the exact race the claim was meant to prevent.

assumption.response:
Assumption hunt on ISSUE-106.md — five realistic options, each with its single reversing premise:

- **A. Sync-before-spend prose rule only** (ISSUE-106.md:14). Load-bearing: a dispatched session actually re-reads CLAUDE.md's Shipping section under spend pressure before executing. If false — sessions run via prompts/subagent dispatch that don't load or override CLAUDE.md (same failure class ADR 0004 flags: "unenforceable... should + poka-yoke is the reachable lever") — option A flips to inadequate, since it's "same enforcement weakness as any prose rule" (the issue says this itself, line 14).

- **B. Claim protocol** (issue comment / assignment, ISSUE-106.md:15). Load-bearing: sessions are granted permission to write to the issue tracker. The issue itself names the reverse case — "dispatch instructions sometimes withhold ('don't change the issues')" (line 15). If that withholding is common rather than occasional, B is non-viable as specified and needs a different claim channel (e.g., a local lock/branch convention) to survive.

- **C. Both (sync + claim)** (ISSUE-106.md:16). Load-bearing: the two mechanisms' failure modes are independent, so any collision is caught by at least one — no race window where both checks pass before either session claims. If two sessions can fetch-and-check nearly simultaneously and both pass before either posts a claim (no atomicity between check and claim), C still under-covers and flips toward needing a single atomic gate rather than two prose/comment checks stacked.

- **D. Mechanize the gate** (a script/pre-flight hook enforcing fetch+check+claim before spend — the option the issue's question implies but doesn't spell out). Load-bearing: dispatched sessions all pass through a common, controllable entry point where a check can be inserted and actually enforced. If sessions can start work ad hoc (e.g., a human runs `claude -p "work issue X"` directly, bypassing any wrapper), there's no reachable lever to gate, and D collapses to no better than option A — an unenforceable convention dressed as tooling.

- **E. Do nothing / accept as tolerable** (implied by ISSUE-106.md:6 — "the redundant run happened to pay for itself... but that was luck, not design"). Load-bearing: duplicate-spend incidents stay rare and self-correcting (cheap, or accidentally useful like this one). If a future duplicate is expensive and doesn't pay for itself (e.g., a paid-experiment-scale rerun under ADR 0042's cost-pilot regime), E flips — the luck-based tolerance no longer holds and prevention becomes justified.

artifacts.docs/decisions/0045-sync-before-spend-pr:
---
id: 0045
title: "Sync-before-spend ships as a PR-body gate line, not a claim protocol or dispatch hook"
status: accepted
summary: "Issue #30 ran twice (~$5-8 wasted, self-corrected by luck) because nothing forced a re-sync before spend. Ship sync-before-spend as a required `Sync-check:` PR-body line mirroring ADR 0030's Retrospective line, enforced by extending check-pr-body.mjs's presence gate — reachable now, no new infrastructure. Reject the issue-comment claim protocol (permission + non-atomicity holes ISSUE-106.md names itself) and a pre-flight dispatch hook (no common entry point exists: `claude -p` runs ad hoc across a dozen disconnected harnesses) as premature machinery for a rare, cheap, self-correcting failure class (closes #106)."
---

# 0045 — Sync-before-spend ships as a PR-body gate line, not a claim protocol or dispatch hook

- Date: 2026-07-14
- Owner: PM
- Panel: lean-process-engineer, process-economist, session-operator — verified via two independent adversarial probes (premortem + assumption-audit), standing in for fresh-eyes/red-team.
- Context: A backlog-dispatch session executed issue #30 in full (~$5-8 nested `claude -p` spend) after main already carried its merged fix; the issue-state read was fresh-at-start, stale-by-use, caught only at end-of-session `git fetch` (ISSUE-106.md:6). Self-corrected by luck, not design. Nothing today forces a re-sync immediately before spend or leaves any signal a second session could see (ISSUE-106.md:10).

## Decision
Ship sync-before-spend as a required PR-body line, `Sync-check: <issue#> re-verified open, no closing PR found (<timestamp>)`, produced immediately before opening the PR — the "repeat before final push" half of ISSUE-106.md:14, in the same workflow slot as ADR 0030's `Retrospective:` line. Extend `scripts/check-pr-body.mjs`'s tested presence gate (already wired into `gates.yml`) with a second anchored regex — one more line in an existing, precedented gate, not a new script family. Reject the issue-comment claim protocol and a dispatch pre-flight hook for now (below). House the related lesson (ISSUE-106.md:18, foreground-wait on long child runs) as a CLAUDE.md dispatch-prompt note alongside this line, not separate machinery.

## Justification
Cheapest lever that is still a real gate, not prose: extending `check-pr-body.mjs` is a few lines against an already-tested gate. This closes premortem finding 1 — CLAUDE.md:23's own forcing-function philosophy predicts unenforced prose decays, and this harness's context summarization gives a concrete reason an early-session "repeat before push" instruction erodes by the time a long session actually pushes. It sidesteps premortem finding 2 (dispatch prompts withholding issue-write access) since the session writes into its OWN PR body, the same artifact it already writes `Retrospective:` into — no issue permission needed. Reachable now: it hooks an existing, already-enforced choke point (`gates.yml` on `pull_request`) every PR already passes, unlike a claim protocol or dispatch gate.

## Assumptions
- [verified] `check-pr-body.mjs` is a pure, unit-tested presence predicate (`RETRO_LINE`, :18) wired into `gates.yml` (ADR 0030) — read 2026-07-14; a second anchored regex is the same shape of change.
- [verified] no common dispatch entry point exists to gate: `claude -p` is invoked ad hoc across a dozen disconnected benchmark harnesses plus manual backlog dispatch (ISSUE-106.md:6), not one wrapper — grepped 2026-07-14; a pre-flight hook has nowhere single to attach.
- [checkable] the extended gate fires on a body missing `Sync-check:` and not on one that has it — owner: verifier; a `check-pr-body.test.mjs` case pair, mirroring ADR 0030's existing shape.
- [unverifiable] WEAKEST — duplicate-spend collisions stay rare/cheap enough (this repo's casual backlog-dispatch class, distinct from ADR 0042's already-covered deliberate paid-experiment class) that a self-attested pre-push check, not an atomic lock, is proportionate. REOPEN-IF: a second incident occurs despite this line, or a collision lands inside ADR 0042's cost-pilot regime where the spend no longer self-corrects.

## Rejected alternatives
- Claim protocol (issue comment/assignment, ISSUE-106.md:15) — dispatch prompts sometimes withhold issue-write permission (named in the issue itself); a comment-based claim is check-then-act over a network round-trip, not atomic, so concurrent dispatch can still both pass before either claims (premortem finding 3); a stalled session's orphaned claim (ISSUE-106.md:18) has no named owner to clear it.
- Dispatch pre-flight hook (a wrapper all sessions pass through) — no reachable attach point exists yet (verified above); building that infrastructure first is a separate, larger, unrequested undertaking — gold-plating against CLAUDE.md's own anti-gold-plating line — that one self-correcting incident doesn't yet justify.
- Prose-only, no PR-body line (ISSUE-106.md:14 as scoped) — the issue names this weakness itself; summarization gives a concrete reason the "repeat before final push" half decays exactly when it matters.
- Do nothing — the incident's luck (self-corrected, surfaced #104) is explicitly not a design property (ISSUE-106.md:6); leaves the gap fully open.

## Revisit triggers
- A second duplicate-spend incident occurs with the Sync-check line in place -> escalate to a claim protocol, first solving the issue-write-permission gap centrally rather than per-session.
- A common dispatch entry point gets built for other reasons -> revisit the pre-flight-hook rejection; the reachability premise would flip.
- A collision lands inside ADR 0042's cost-pilot regime -> the self-correcting-luck assumption no longer holds; treat as urgent, not backlog.
